``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} # Parole Eligibility ## How Parole Eligibility is Determined By statute, most people in prison become eligible for discretionary parole release after serving one-third of their judicial maximum sentences with some exceptions. Georgia is one of four states that have no system of good-time or earned-time credits that can alter the lengths of incarcerated people's minimum or maximum terms.
## In Prison Past Their Parole Eligibility Year In 2020, there were 11,827 people in prison past their parole eligibility date. This group made up 46% of people in prison for new crimes and with sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Demographics The demographics of people in prison past their parole eligibility year reveals notable proportions among non-Hispanic Black and White people. Gender distribution indicates a predominance of males over females. Age-wise, the majority of individuals fell within the 25-34 years and 35-44 years brackets. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Criminal Offense ::: {.cell} ::: In 2020, 61% of people in prison past their parole eligibility date were in prison for violent offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: The breakdown of criminal offenses of people in prison past their parole eligibility year reveals a varied landscape, with the majority of people incarcerated for aggravated or simple assault (26%) and property crimes (19%). Other offense categories make up smaller proportions of this population. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Sentence Length In 2020, among the prison population eligible for parole but not yet released, people with sentences between 10 to 24.9 years constituted the majority, representing 66%. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Missing Data This section will have information about missing parole eligibility data for the state once new NCRP data is released.
# Prison Population ## Prison Population Trends From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 17%, changing from 56,432 in 2010 to 47,010 in 2022. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: Source: BJS Prisoners Series
## Demographics ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: Source: BJS Prisoners 2022 - Race and Ethnicity
## Criminal Offenses ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Sentence Lengths ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
# Prison Releases ## Prison Release Trends From 2010 to 2020, prison releases decreased 16%, dropping from 21,717 in 2010 to 18,298 in 2020. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
The proportions of parole-eligible individuals released versus those not released have shown fluctuating trends from 2010 to 2020. In 2020, 40% of parole-eligible individuals were released, while 60% remained in prison, reflecting ongoing challenges and changes in parole policies and practices. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Conditional Releases Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 45% of releases are conditional, requiring ongoing supervision and compliance, while 55% are unconditional, allowing individuals to reintegrate without additional constraints. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Demographics of People Released The demographics of people released from prison in 2020 reveals notable proportions among non-Hispanic Black and White people. Gender distribution indicates a predominance of males over females. Age-wise, the majority of individuals fall within the 25-34 years and 35-44 years brackets. These findings provide insights into the populations transitioning back into the community. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Time Served by Offense Between 2010 and 2020, shifts in average time served by individuals for different offense types have been observed. While some categories, like murder and non-negligent manslaughter, have seen a substantial increase in time served, others, like drug-related offenses, have remained relatively stable. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
# Disparities ## Incarceration Rates In Georgia, Black, non-Hispanic people are incarcerated in state prison at a rate 3 times higher than White, non-Hispanic people, when accounting for population sizes in the community. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Years Spent in Prison After Parole Eligibility In 2020, people in prison past their parole eligibility year collectively spent a total of 5,388 years behind bars instead of supervised in the community on parole. This extended incarceration varied across different racial and ethnic groups. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

## Timing of Release After Parole Eligibility The timing of release for individuals after their parole eligibility year varied by offense type and demographic group, with many remaining in prison for several years beyond their eligibility date. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Time Served ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Hispanic individuals faced the longest average length of stay in prison in 2020. Similarly, Black, non-Hispanic individuals also experienced notably longer prison stays compared to their White, non-Hispanic counterparts, who had the shortest average length of stay.
